status_notes.note: Public operator support is now explicit and excludes facade authoring helpers other than get_schema().

adrs.summary: The older operator-surface ADR was too coarse and did not distinguish between facade-exported authoring helpers and explicitly supported public operator surfaces.
adrs.title: Public operator surface boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl uses the word "schema" in multiple domains. Without a dedicated namespace-boundary ADR, helper and DDL docs drift into ambiguity between Pydantic/model schema materialization and database namespace preparation.
adrs.title: Database namespace boundary

adrs.summary: The active line currently exposes overlapping exchange tokens across Python bindings, runtime adapters, Rust specs, and draft architecture notes.
adrs.title: Canonical exchange vocabulary, aliasing, and provisional token policy

adrs.summary: The active line already uses `POST_EMIT` as a real completion fence, but the public hook phase vocabulary stops at `POST_RESPONSE`.
adrs.title: Transport completion fence and POST_EMIT legality

adrs.summary: Tigrbl already distinguishes user-facing runtime/operator contracts from lower-layer spec/base authorities, but repo-local maintenance workflows need a separate boundary so maintainers can evolve them without widening the supported user-facing surface area.
adrs.title: Private maintainer surface boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl exposes several top-level helper functions for composing tables, hooks, handlers, schemas, and routers. Those helpers are useful for framework authoring and assembly, but they are not the same category of support as stable operator-facing transport, docs, or request/response contracts.
adrs.title: Private authoring surface boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests exercise operation declarations attached through internal decorators, external binding, app and router owners, plain objects, multiple table targets, and dynamic attachment after owner construction. Existing ADRs already say decorators are declarative sugar and that specs are authoritative, but they do not explicitly freeze the placement boundary for operation declarations.
adrs.title: Op declaration attachment boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover aliasing canonical verbs, preserving canonical core handlers, overriding canonical behavior through explicit aliases, and using custom aliases and targets. Existing shortcut and alias ADRs establish that aliases expand to canonical forms, but the tests reveal a sharper architectural line: alias is exposure identity, target is semantic identity.
adrs.title: Alias versus target resolution boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover persist overrides, token normalization, custom-target persist behavior, and handler-chain ordering. Existing ADRs discuss database guardrails and canonical operation semantics, but persist policy needs an explicit boundary so future transport or decorator changes do not reinterpret persistence behavior locally.
adrs.title: Op persist policy and handler chain boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover REST default op verbs, REST/RPC parity for default operations, `create` versus `bulk_create`, `clear` versus `bulk_delete`, and non-bulk-capable fallback behavior. Existing ADRs freeze the default canonical OLTP set and REST/RPC parity, but they do not explicitly decide how default-operation collisions and capability gaps are resolved.
adrs.title: Default op collision and capability precedence

adrs.summary: operation contract tests exercise `OpSpec` effects across schemas, columns, defaults, storage, REST routes, RPC methods, core CRUD handlers, hooks, atom injection, and system-step registry behavior. Existing ADRs establish a spec-first architecture and one semantic operation model, but they do not explicitly name the materialization side-effect boundary that these tests rely on.
adrs.title: OpSpec materialization side-effect boundary

status_notes.note: Accepted transport bypass removal update for compiled mapping dispatch and boundary evidence.

adrs.summary: The transport boundary tests assert that old `tigrbl.transport` modules, dispatcher files, gateway runtime bypasses, and top-level dispatch exports remain absent. Existing KernelPlan dispatch ADRs define the positive owner of dispatch, but the tests also need a negative boundary decision that makes reintroduction of old bypass surfaces explicitly non-conforming.
adrs.title: Transport bypass removal boundary

adrs.summary: The transport-dispatch mapping harness tests freeze a deterministic plan order and assert that default canonical operations become visible from collected model declarations. Existing kernel-plan ADRs govern runtime compilation broadly, but this test concern is earlier in the pipeline: mapping model declarations into normalized operation and binding materialization inputs.
adrs.title: Mapping plan compilation boundary

status_notes.note: Updated from legacy package edge test inventory to make pre-TX dependency edge-row verification explicit.

adrs.summary: The pre-transaction dependency tests assert that `secdeps` and `deps` execute in `PRE_TX_BEGIN`, that failures abort before `START_TX`, and that kernel diagnostics expose dependency placement. Existing dependency ordering and database guardrail ADRs cover the broad intent, but the tests rely on a sharper phase and transaction boundary.
adrs.title: Pre-TX dependency execution boundary

adrs.summary: The project already treats registry and release artifacts as canonical JSON data, but it does not yet declare RFC 8785 JCS as the required canonicalization profile for proof-oriented payloads. Without an explicit boundary, serializer drift can produce byte-level instability across runtimes and versions.
adrs.title: RFC 8785 JCS canonicalization boundary

status_notes.note: Updated from example, performance, and options test inventory to make docs/runtime parity explicit for system, diagnostics, OpenAPI, and security projections.

adrs.summary: The example test suite cover OpenAPI path exposure, system endpoint visibility, security scheme projection, diagnostics mounts, Uvicorn runtime parity, and client-visible REST/RPC behavior. Those tests reflect a documentation architecture where docs are generated from canonical metadata and materialized framework state, then checked against runtime behavior.
adrs.title: Documentation support surface partitioning

adrs.summary: Persistence, ORM, DBAPI, SQL text, table names, bound parameters, and database driver details must never be exposed through external transport responses.
adrs.title: External Error Detail Sanitization Boundary

adrs.summary: Protocol growth across REST, JSON-RPC, stream, SSE, WebSocket, WebTransport, static, lifespan, callbacks, and webhooks requires a stable split between wire input, semantic runtime classification, and atom execution.
adrs.title: Contract-derived runtime event vocabulary

adrs.summary: Current runtime channels expose coarse subevents. The target protocol model requires request, response, session, message, stream, and datagram leaves to express ordering and loop semantics without creating protocol-specific phase systems.
adrs.title: Runtime subevent expansion boundary

adrs.summary: Stream, SSE, WebSocket, WebTransport, callbacks, and webhooks require branching, repeated regions, and error paths that cannot be represented faithfully by a single success-only phase list.
adrs.title: Protocol phase trees and ok-err branching

adrs.summary: Response-level iteration is insufficient for event ordering, cancellation, backpressure, and executor parity across protocols.
adrs.title: Loop regions for stream SSE WebSocket and WebTransport

adrs.summary: Protocol-specific response send behavior is currently adapter-backed. The target model needs transport emission to be visible to KernelPlan ordering, diagnostics, and conformance evidence.
adrs.title: Transport emit atom boundary

adrs.summary: WebTransport is currently visible as a binding/spec surface but remains provisional in support-level governance. The target protocol set requires all tigr-asgi-contract WebTransport subevents to be represented explicitly.
adrs.title: WebTransport session stream and datagram runtime boundary

adrs.summary: Static files and lifespan currently have special handling relative to ordinary operation execution. The target protocol inventory requires clear phase-chain or explicit bypass authority for both.
adrs.title: Static and lifespan runtime inclusion boundary

adrs.summary: The target eventful runtime needs continuation behavior without overloading hooks or hiding callback execution inside handlers.
adrs.title: First-class callback runtime boundary

adrs.summary: The target runtime needs outbound event delivery without conflating it with in-process hooks or continuation callbacks.
adrs.title: First-class webhook delivery boundary

adrs.summary: The target architecture depends on compiling atoms, kernel plans, phase chains, and phase trees from BindingSpecs and tigr-asgi-contract-derived semantics.

issues.description: JSON-RPC table-backed CRUD dispatch allows Thing.create-style requests with missing required create params to reach persistence, causing database NOT NULL failures and leaking SQLAlchemy/DBAPI details, SQL text, table/column names, and bound parameters in the JSON-RPC -32602 response. Framework must validate generated operation input before persistence and sanitize persistence exceptions at the JSON-RPC boundary.
issues.title: JSON-RPC create with missing required fields leaks persistence internals

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, /openapi.json exposes generated CRUD operations with components.securitySchemes absent and zero operations carrying security requirements. Runtime and contract policy must agree: protected operations should emit security schemes and requirements; public operations should be explicitly limited and documented.
issues.title: OpenAPI generated operations do not declare authentication

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, /openrpc.json exposes generated JSON-RPC methods with an empty components.securitySchemes object and zero methods carrying security requirements. Runtime and OpenRPC contract policy must agree: protected methods should declare the security scheme and requirement; public methods should be explicitly identified as unauthenticated.
issues.title: OpenRPC generated methods do not declare authentication

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, GET /tenant without an Authorization header returns HTTP 200 OK with tenant records. If generated CRUD routes are protected by default, the request should fail with 401 or 403. If generated CRUD routes are public by default, that public policy must be explicit in the generated contract.
issues.title: Generated REST read allows unauthenticated access

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, POST /rpc calling Tenant.list without an Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with a JSON-RPC result payload. If generated JSON-RPC methods are protected by default, unauthenticated calls should fail with an authentication or authorization error. If generated JSON-RPC methods are public by default, that public policy must be explicit in OpenRPC.
issues.title: Generated JSON-RPC read allows unauthenticated access
